one_line_takeaway: A structured four-axis (Why/What/Where/How) survey of LLM safety evaluation that catalogs 31 benchmarks and 7 frameworks and argues the field's core problem is the absence of a unified, reproducible definition of 'attack success'.
key_finding: The field lacks a unified, reproducible evaluation paradigm: even the most fundamental metric, Attack Success Rate, has no agreed definition of what counts as a 'successful' jailbreak, so rule-based methods correlate poorly with human judgment while model-based judges are flexible but non-reproducible across studies. The survey argues this fragmentation (small datasets, inconsistent setups, domain-specific requirements) undermines comparability, and proposes standardized reporting protocols, unified metric formulations, and modular/dynamic evaluation as the path forward. (Note: the abstract's Llama2 'exaggerated safety' result is NOT this paper's finding — it is cited from Rottger et al.'s XSTest [ref 32], which showed Llama2 refuses benign queries and that adding guardrail prompts worsens over-defensiveness.)
core_contribution: A survey (not an empirical study) that proposes a four-dimensional taxonomy for organizing the literature on safety evaluation of LLMs: (i) Why to evaluate (motivation, distinction from general-capability evaluation), (ii) What to evaluate (safety task dimensions: toxicity, robustness, ethics, bias & fairness, truthfulness, and an 'other' bucket covering privacy/medical/cyber/code safety), (iii) Where to evaluate (metrics, datasets, benchmarks), and (iv) How to evaluate (human-based, rule-based, and model-based methods plus integrated evaluation frameworks). The authors' own consolidation work includes a comparative feature matrix positioning this survey against five prior surveys (Table I), a categorized catalog of 31 comprehensive safety datasets/benchmarks grouped into Q-only, Q&A, MCQ, and Dialogue formats (Table II), and a component-level comparison of seven evaluation frameworks (Table III). They also enumerate open challenges and future directions (unified evaluation, dynamic evaluation, reliable & efficient evaluators).
threat_model: As a survey the paper defines no single threat model of its own; instead it catalogs the threat surface that safety evaluations target. Adversaries in the surveyed literature use jailbreak attacks and prompt-injection attacks to bypass alignment and elicit unsafe outputs (toxicity, bias, misinformation, privacy leakage, dangerous/cyber/medical content). The paper frames safety evaluation as the systematic process of measuring whether a model resists harmful instructions, avoids unsafe outputs, and stays robust under both benign and adversarial interaction. It notably also frames 'exaggerated safety' / over-defensiveness (refusing benign prompts) as a failure mode to be evaluated, not just under-refusal.
limitations: It is a descriptive survey, so it contributes organization and synthesis rather than new evidence or head-to-head measurements; it does not empirically validate any benchmark or reconcile the conflicting ASR definitions it criticizes. Coverage is a snapshot of a fast-moving field (arXiv v2, Oct 2025). The taxonomy boundaries are fuzzy (e.g., a broad 'Other' category for privacy/medical/cyber/code safety), and the metrics discussion explicitly defers truthfulness/hallucination metrics to prior hallucination surveys rather than covering them.

datasets_used: No datasets were run by the authors; the survey catalogs 31 safety benchmarks in Table II, including: Chu et al. (160 prompts, 16 safety dimensions, English, harmful prompts), R-Judge (162, 27 dimensions, multi-turn agent interaction), JailbreakBench (200, 10 dimensions, harmful+benign behaviors)

Other catalogued benchmarks/datasets include StrongREJECT, HarmBench, Do-Not-Answer, AdvBench, SORRY-Bench, SALAD-Bench, ALERT, WildGuardMix, SafetyBench, S-Eval, XSafety, ToxiGen, DecodingTrust, and XSTest (cited, not run)
quantitative_results: Taxonomy spans 4 dimensions (Why / What / Where / How to evaluate)
Table II: 31 comprehensive datasets/benchmarks selected and grouped into 4 composition types (Q-only, Q&A, MCQ, Dialogue)
Table I: this survey compared against 5 prior surveys across 7 coverage criteria; 'Ours' is the only one marked as covering all seven

Table III framework component counts (Judges/Attacks/Defenses/Interfaces): JailbreakEval = 22 judges (no attacks/defenses/interfaces); EasyJailbreak = 12 attacks / 7 judges / 4 interfaces; WalledEval = 9 attacks / 6 judges / 6 interfaces (35+ built-in benchmarks); HarmBench = 18 attacks / 3 judges / 5 interfaces; JailbreakBench = 4 attacks / 5 defenses / 6 judges / 3 interfaces; AISafetyLab = 13 attacks / 16 defenses / 7 judges / 3 interfaces; PandaGuard = 19 attacks / 12 defenses / 4 judges / 7 interfaces

Metric ASR defined as ASR = Num_success / Num_total; fine-grained alternatives surveyed include Cai et al.'s three metrics — Safeguard Violation (SV), Informativeness (I), Relative Truthfulness (RT) — and StrongREJECT's three-score chain-of-thought scheme
baselines_compared: Prior surveys positioned against in Table I: Guo et al. 2023, Dong et al. 2024, Yi et al. 2024, Ran et al. 2024, Rottger et al. 2025 (this is survey-vs-survey feature comparison, not experimental baselines)
